title: Granny square
subtitle: The canonical Victorian motif, four rounds
excerpt: The four-round granny square — clusters of treble at the corners and along the sides, separated by chain spaces. Worked in DK cotton on a 4 mm hook, each finished motif measures 10 × 10 cm and joins up to make blankets, cushion covers, and bags.
sourceNotes: Weldon's Practical Crochet, Volume 1 (1885) — the earliest English-language pattern for the four-round square-from-the-centre motif (Internet Archive). Beeton's Book of Needlework (1870) — corner construction cross-reference. Therese de Dillmont, Encyclopedia of Needlework (1886) — chain-space spacing and joining method.

crochet.gaugeText: Each finished granny square measures 10 × 10 cm in DK-weight cotton with a 4 mm hook, after a light steam-block. Tension is moderate; if your square comes out smaller than 9 cm, try a 4.5 mm hook.
crochet.finishedSizeText: Each motif: 10 × 10 cm. The pattern below makes one motif; multiply for blankets, cushion covers, or bags.

chartDefinition.title: Granny square — all four rounds
chartDefinition.caption: The chart reads outward from the magic ring at the centre. Corner chain-2 spaces sit between the two clusters that form each corner; side chain-1 spaces sit between cluster pairs along the sides.

recipeTools.notes: For sewing in tails and (optionally) joining squares with a whip-stitch seam.
Useful on round 4 to mark the start of the round when you stop mid-square and pick up later.
For pinning the finished square to shape if it comes off the hook slightly skewed.

glossaryTerms.definition: The gap created by skipping a chain or chains. In the granny square, the corner is a chain-2 space between the two clusters; the side spaces between clusters are chain-1 spaces.
Three trebles worked into the same chain-space, joined at the top. The basic building block of the granny square.

content.text: The granny square is the canonical Victorian motif — Weldon's Practical Crochet shows it in its 1885 volume, in DK-weight cotton with a 4 mm hook, four rounds from the magic ring at the centre to the finished 10 × 10 cm square. The construction is simple enough to learn in an afternoon and rich enough to underpin a blanket, a cushion cover, a tote bag, a market shawl. The 1970s revival made the granny famous as a scrap-yarn motif; the Victorian original used remnants of mercerised cotton in the same way.

Each round of the granny square adds one row of trebles around the outside of the previous round, with the same anchor: three trebles into each chain-space, two chains across each corner, one chain between cluster pairs along the sides. Once the structure clicks, you can crochet a granny in front of the television without looking at the chart.
If you haven't worked a treble or a magic ring before, pause and read the
Treble and the magic ring
STITCH tutorial first. The granny assumes both moves are familiar.

attrs.title: Materials
attrs.body: For one motif: about 5 g of DK-weight cotton (you can use up to three colours; this pattern shows the classic single-colour version, with notes on colour-changes below). 4 mm crochet hook (UK 8, US G/6). Tapestry needle for sewing in ends. Optional: a stitch marker for marking the start of round 4.

content.text: Gauge

Each finished granny square measures 10 × 10 cm in DK-weight cotton with a 4 mm hook, after a light steam-block. Tension is moderate; if your square comes out smaller than 9 cm, try a 4.5 mm hook. If it comes out larger than 11 cm, try a 3.5 mm hook. For blanket-scale projects, work one motif before starting the rest — a 5% error per motif compounds quickly across 80 squares.
Stitches used
chain (ch) — UK and US shared.
slip stitch (sl st) — UK and US shared.
treble (tr) — UK convention; the same stitch as US "double crochet".
magic ring (MR) — the adjustable starting loop that lets the square begin without a centre hole.
Pattern

Worked in the round, joined at the end of each round with a slip stitch into the top of the starting chain. Right side faces you throughout — do not turn at the end of a round. Numbers in brackets at the end of each round are the running stitch count.
The 3-chain turning chain at the start of each round counts as the first treble of the first
cluster
Round 1

Form a magic ring. Ch 3 (counts as the first tr). Work 2 tr into the ring. *Ch 2, 3 tr into the ring*; repeat from * to * twice more. Ch 2, sl st to top of beginning ch-3 to join. Pull the tail to close the ring. (12 tr, 4 ch-2 corner spaces.)
Round 2
Sl st across the top of the next 2 tr and into the first
chain-space

. Ch 3 (counts as the first tr). Into the same ch-2 corner sp, work 2 tr, ch 2, 3 tr. *Ch 1, into the next ch-2 corner sp work (3 tr, ch 2, 3 tr)*; repeat from * to * twice more. Ch 1, sl st to top of beginning ch-3 to join. (24 tr, 4 ch-2 corner spaces, 4 ch-1 side spaces.)
Round 3

Sl st across the top of the next 2 tr and into the first ch-2 corner sp. Ch 3 (counts as the first tr). Into the same ch-2 corner sp, work 2 tr, ch 2, 3 tr. Ch 1. *Into the next ch-1 side sp, work 3 tr. Ch 1. Into the next ch-2 corner sp, work (3 tr, ch 2, 3 tr). Ch 1.* Repeat from * to * twice more. Into the next ch-1 side sp, work 3 tr. Ch 1. Sl st to top of beginning ch-3 to join. (36 tr, 4 ch-2 corner spaces, 8 ch-1 side spaces.)
Round 4

Sl st across the top of the next 2 tr and into the first ch-2 corner sp. Ch 3 (counts as the first tr). Into the same ch-2 corner sp, work 2 tr, ch 2, 3 tr. Ch 1. *Into each of the next 2 ch-1 side spaces, work 3 tr separated by ch 1 (so: 3 tr, ch 1, 3 tr, ch 1). Into the next ch-2 corner sp, work (3 tr, ch 2, 3 tr). Ch 1.* Repeat from * to * twice more. Into each of the remaining 2 ch-1 side spaces, work 3 tr separated by ch 1 (3 tr, ch 1, 3 tr, ch 1). Sl st to top of beginning ch-3 to join. (48 tr, 4 ch-2 corner spaces, 12 ch-1 side spaces.)
Chart

The chart shows all four rounds at once, in working order from the centre outward. Read each round anti-clockwise from the slip-stitch join. The corner clusters meet at a chain-2 space; the side clusters meet at a chain-1 space. Round 4 carries the abbreviation "3tr × 16" because the pattern repeats — sixteen 3-treble clusters around the round, alternating chain-1 (side) and chain-2 (corner) spaces.

definition.title: Granny square — all four rounds
definition.caption: The chart reads outward from the magic ring at the centre. Corner chain-2 spaces sit between the two clusters that form each corner; side chain-1 spaces sit between cluster pairs along the sides.

content.text: Finishing

Fasten off. Leave a 15 cm tail for sewing in. Thread the tail through a tapestry needle and weave through 3-4 stitches along the back of round 4, then back through 2-3 stitches in the opposite direction. Trim. The starting tail (from the magic ring) is woven in the same way along the back of round 1.

Blocking is optional for a single motif but worth doing on any piece that will become a finished item. Wet-block: dip the square briefly in cool water, gently squeeze out (don't wring), pat between two clean towels, then pin to a blocking mat at the four corners and along the sides so the square measures 10 × 10 cm with right angles at each corner. Leave to dry. Steam-block: hold the steam iron 2-3 cm above the pinned square, hover (don't press) for 10-15 seconds, leave pinned until cool.
Joining motifs into a finished piece

For blankets and cushion covers, lay the blocked squares right-sides-up in the arrangement you want before joining — the joining order matters less than getting the colour layout right. The traditional Weldon's join is a whip-stitch with a tapestry needle through the back loops only; the modern join-as-you-go method works the joining slip stitch into the last round of each new square. Either approach works; whip-stitch is the slower-but-tidier choice for a first blanket, join-as-you-go saves time at the cost of one extra round of focus per square.

Once joined, the assembled piece gets a final blocking. For a blanket of 80 squares (8 × 10 grid), the finished size after blocking is about 80 × 100 cm — a lap blanket, scaled to whatever larger size the maker chooses.
Care

DK cotton washes well at 30°C on a wool-or-handwash cycle, or by hand in cool water with a small amount of mild soap. Don't tumble-dry — cotton shrinks under hot air. Lay flat on a clean towel to dry, smoothing the piece back to shape.
What to try next

Once the four-round granny is reliable, the same construction extends to as many rounds as you want — six rounds gives a 15 × 15 cm motif; eight rounds gives a 20 × 20 cm motif large enough that one square is a small cushion-cover front. The granny-hexagon (six corners instead of four, three trebles between each corner cluster) is the next motif in the family; the canonical Weldon's lace doily is a granny structure with chain spaces stretched out across the sides to make openwork.
